subtitle: Deep lower calf — slow to heal, often underestimated

strava_risk_triggers.risk_note: Soleus is loaded more at slower paces and longer distances than gastrocnemius

mild.description: Deep aching in the lower calf, worse going upstairs
mild.can_run_note: Yes — avoid hills, include bent-knee calf stretches
why: Soleus fatigue from sustained load during long slow runs
Bent-knee loading during uphill running stressing the soleus specifically
relief: Bent-knee calf stretch: wall lean with knee bent 60s each side — targets soleus specifically
Heat on lower calf before runs
prevention: Eccentric calf raises with bent knee — isolates the soleus
Adequate rest between marathon-distance long runs

moderate.description: Persistent deep lower calf ache, tender to squeeze, worse after runs
moderate.can_run_note: Flat easy runs only — no hills
why: Soleus strain or tendinopathy at the Achilles-soleus junction
Stress reaction in the tibia or fibula adjacent to the soleus attachment
Posterior compartment syndrome in high-mileage runners
relief: Bent-knee eccentric calf raises — load the soleus eccentrically
prevention: Soleus-specific strengthening in the training programme
Avoid sudden increase in uphill or trail running
Monitor for tibial pain alongside calf pain

severe.description: Deep calf pain at rest, severe after any running, possible numbness
severe.can_run_note: Do not run — soleus injuries become chronic if undertreated
why: Possible stress fracture of the tibia or fibula — needs imaging
relief: MRI or bone scan if stress fracture suspected
Compartment pressure testing if pain is severe and diffuse
prevention: Full rehabilitation before return — soleus injuries frequently become chronic
Progressive loading programme under physio guidance

see_physio_if: Deep bone-like pain in the lower leg
Calf pain that does not improve with rest
Tightness and pain that come on at a consistent point in every run
No improvement after 1 week of rest
